title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
text:
Visualisation — Tableau
Sheet 1 — OF Artist Ranking uses task3_artist_notoriety as its data source, with Performer Name on Rows, SUM(Total Notable Works) on Columns, and two filters applied: Is Oceanus Folk Artist = True and SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2. This produces a horizontal bar chart ranking all 14 Oceanus Folk artists with two or more charted works, sorted in descending order by total works. The chart establishes the candidate pool for rising star prediction: Orla Seabloom leads with 7 notable works, followed by Copper Canyon Ghosts (5), Daniel O’Connell (4), Beatrice Albright (4), and Ping Meng (4). The remaining nine artists each have exactly 2 notable works.
Sheet 2 — Genre Mix by Candidate uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Artist Name on Rows, CNT(Work Name) on Columns, and Genre on the Colour mark. A filter on CNT(Work Name) restricts the view to the top five candidates with the highest work counts. This stacked bar chart reveals the genre composition of each candidate’s notable output. All top candidates produce exclusively Oceanus Folk, with the exception of Ping Meng who has two Lo-Fi Electronica works and one Dream Pop work alongside a single Oceanus Folk work — identifying her as a genre-diverse outlier rather than a pure Oceanus Folk rising star.
Sheet 3 — Notoriety Timeline uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Notoriety Date on Columns (treated as a continuous dimension), Artist Name on Rows, Artist Name on the Colour mark, and Work Name on the Detail mark to plot one dot per notable work. A filter on SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2 retains the full 14-candidate pool. Rows are arranged so that artists with later career activity appear toward the top. This dot plot reveals the temporal distribution of each artist’s chart appearances — distinguishing between artists who achieved notoriety in a concentrated burst (Orla Seabloom with all 7 works charting in 2029, Beatrice Albright with 4 works in 2031) and those whose careers span many years (Ping Meng from 2011 to 2028, Tao Yin from 2013 to 2029).
Sheet 4 — Three Artist Comparison uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Notoriety Date on Columns (continuous), Artist Name and Work Name on Rows, and Artist Name on the Colour mark. An Artist Name filter selects only the three predicted rising stars: Copper Canyon Ghosts, Beatrice Albright, and Orla Seabloom. This sheet provides a detailed work-by-work comparison of each prediction’s career trajectory, showing the title and chart year of every notable work side by side.
Sheet 5 — Breakthrough Profile uses task3_artist_notoriety as its data source, with Years Active Span on Columns, SUM(Total Notable Works) on Rows, Performer Name on the Label mark, and a Circle mark type. Filters are Is Oceanus Folk Artist = True and SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2. The x-axis begins at 0. This scatter plot positions each of the 14 candidates by how long they were active before charting (x-axis) against how many works they have charted (y-axis). Artists in the top-left region — high output with a short active span — represent the ideal rising star profile. Orla Seabloom occupies this position most strongly (7 works, 0-year span), while Ping Meng sits as an extreme outlier in the top-right (4 works, 17-year span), indicating a long and slow career rather than a rapid breakout.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
text:
Visualisation — Python Radar Chart



Rising Star Profile — Oceanus Folk Candidates: radar chart comparing top 5 candidates across five normalised dimensions with companion data table


A radar chart was generated in Python using matplotlib (Data/task3_radar.py) to compare the top five Oceanus Folk candidates across five normalised dimensions simultaneously — a multi-axis comparison that cannot be achieved natively in Tableau.
The top five candidates by total_notable_works were selected from task3_artist_notoriety: Orla Seabloom (7), Copper Canyon Ghosts (5), Daniel O’Connell (4), Beatrice Albright (4), and Ping Meng (4).
Five metrics were defined and min–max normalised within this five-artist pool:








Metric
Field
Direction




Total Notable Works
total_notable_works
Higher = better


Quick Breakthrough
years_active_span (inverted)
Lower span = higher score


Early Breakthrough
first_notoriety_date (inverted)
Earlier year = higher score


Recent Activity
latest_notoriety_date
Later year = higher score


Genre Diversity
Count of unique genres in genres field
More genres = higher score



Normalisation was scoped to the five-candidate pool rather than the full dataset, so each radar axis represents relative standing among the top candidates. The chart was saved as a PNG at 150 dpi and embedded as a static image in the Tableau dashboard alongside a companion data table showing the raw values.

Together, these three views answer the question of which Oceanus Folk artists form the candidate pool and how they compare on output volume, genre focus, and breakthrough speed.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
text:
Results and Discussion

What does it mean to be a rising star?
Based on the visualisations above, a rising star in the Oceanus Folk music scene is characterised by five attributes:

High notable output — the artist has multiple works that achieved chart notoriety, not just a single hit. Among the 56 Oceanus Folk artists in the dataset, only 14 produced two or more notable works, and the strongest candidates produced four or more.
Rapid breakthrough — the time between the artist’s first release and their first chart appearance is short. Orla Seabloom and Beatrice Albright both have a years-active span of zero, meaning their works charted in the same year they were released. Copper Canyon Ghosts achieved a span of just one year. By contrast, Ping Meng’s 17-year span and Tao Yin’s 16-year span suggest established careers rather than breakout trajectories.
Concentrated momentum — rising stars tend to chart multiple works within a narrow time window rather than sporadically over many years. Orla Seabloom charted all 7 works in 2029; Beatrice Albright charted all 4 in 2031. This burst pattern signals a career inflection point — the hallmark of a rising star.
Genre commitment — the strongest Oceanus Folk candidates produce almost exclusively within the genre. Ping Meng, despite having 4 notable works, splits across Lo-Fi Electronica, Dream Pop, and Oceanus Folk — making her more of a genre-crossing veteran than a rising Oceanus Folk star.
Recency — artists whose chart activity is more recent are more likely to represent the next wave. Copper Canyon Ghosts’ notoriety dates (2034–2035) are the most recent in the candidate pool, placing them at the frontier of the genre’s evolution.

The Breakthrough Profile scatter plot captures attributes 1 and 2 directly: ideal candidates cluster in the top-left (high works, low span). The Notoriety Timeline captures attribute 3 visually through dot clustering. The Genre Mix chart captures attribute 4 through colour segmentation. The radar chart synthesises all five attributes into a single comparative shape — a larger, more balanced polygon indicates a stronger overall rising star profile.


Three Artist Comparison
The three artists selected for detailed comparison are Orla Seabloom, Beatrice Albright, and Copper Canyon Ghosts. Each represents a distinct breakout pattern:
Orla Seabloom had the most explosive debut in the candidate pool — all 7 of her notable works (Beneath the Whispering Waves, Ebbing Heart, Riding the Moon’s Reflection, Seasheel Lullaby, The Shifting Sands, The Song of Forgotten Shores, and Tidal Journey) charted in a single year (2029). Her radar profile is the largest overall shape, dominating the Total Notable Works axis and scoring maximally on Quick Breakthrough. However, because all works charted in the same year, her Recent Activity score is moderate — her last chart appearance was 2029, earlier than the other two predictions.
Beatrice Albright followed a similar concentrated pattern, charting 4 works (Moonlit Kelp Forest Dance, Seven Fathoms Deep, The Siren’s Sigh, Whispers on the Jetty Wind) in 2031, all in Oceanus Folk. Her years-active span is also zero, giving her a strong Quick Breakthrough score. Her profile closely mirrors Orla Seabloom’s shape on the radar chart, albeit smaller due to fewer total works.
Copper Canyon Ghosts charted 5 works across 2034–2035 (Canyon Echoes, Ocean Dreams; Crimson Cliffs Awakening; Salt-Stained Letters; Shadows in Smuggler’s Cove; The Cartographer’s Curse), all in Oceanus Folk. With only a 1-year span and the most recent notoriety dates in the entire candidate pool, Copper Canyon Ghosts score highest on Recent Activity. Their radar profile is the most balanced of the three, with strong scores across all axes except Early Breakthrough (since their career began later).


Predictions — Next Three Oceanus Folk Stars
Based on the rising star profile defined above, the three artists predicted to be the next Oceanus Folk breakout stars over the next five years are:

Copper Canyon Ghosts — Their 5 notable works between 2034 and 2035, combined with a 1-year active span and pure Oceanus Folk output, make them the most recent breakout act in the candidate pool. Recency is a decisive factor for a forward-looking prediction: their chart activity is the newest of any candidate, giving them the strongest momentum heading into the next five years.
Orla Seabloom — With 7 notable works and an instant breakthrough in 2029, she has the highest volume and fastest rise of any Oceanus Folk candidate. Her concentrated output suggests an artist at the peak of a career inflection, and her exclusive focus on Oceanus Folk makes her the genre’s most prolific emerging voice. She ranks second because her chart activity, while impressive, is five years older than Copper Canyon Ghosts’.
Beatrice Albright — With 4 works charting simultaneously in 2031 and zero years-active span, she demonstrates the same explosive breakout pattern as Orla Seabloom. Her exclusively Oceanus Folk catalogue and rapid rise make her a strong candidate to sustain chart presence in the coming years.

These three were chosen over Daniel O’Connell (who matches Beatrice Albright’s numbers but shares the same 2031 burst, making them interchangeable on the available metrics) and Ping Meng (whose 17-year span and multi-genre output disqualify her from the rising star profile despite having 4 notable works).

title: Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread
text:
Genre Influence Flow



Genres Most Influenced by Oceanus Folk (left) and Genres That Inspire Oceanus Folk (right) — horizontal stacked bar charts coloured by edge type



Left chart — Genres Most Influenced by Oceanus Folk
A horizontal stacked bar chart showing target genres ranked by total connection count. Each color segment represents a different type of musical influence. Longer bar = stronger influence from Oceanus Folk on that genre. Each segment shows how the influence was expressed: CoverOf (Oceanus Folk songs were covered by this genre), DirectlySamples (Oceanus Folk audio was directly sampled), InStyleOf (works were created in the style of Oceanus Folk), InterpolatesFrom (melodies were borrowed from Oceanus Folk), LyricalReferenceTo (lyrics referenced Oceanus Folk works). The genres are sorted from most to least influenced — Indie Folk at the top is the most influenced genre.


Right chart — Genres That Inspire Oceanus Folk
Same chart structure but showing which genres feed INTO Oceanus Folk. If a genre appears prominently on both sides it means there is a two-way musical exchange with Oceanus Folk. Desert Rock, Dream Pop and Indie Folk appearing on both sides confirms they have a bidirectional relationship with Oceanus Folk.

title: Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread
text:
Influence Through Time



Oceanus Folk Outward Influence Over Time (top) and Other Genres Drawing from Oceanus Folk Over Time (bottom) — both annotated with Sailor’s career milestones



Top chart — Oceanus Folk Outward Influence Over Time
A line chart where each point represents the number of influence connections made by Oceanus Folk works in that year. Higher points mean more Oceanus Folk works were influencing other genres that year. Two key events are marked directly on the chart: “Sailor joins Ivy Echoes” (2023) — marks the dramatic spike in outward influence; “Sailor’s global breakthrough” (2028) — marks the second wave of sustained influence. The overall pattern shows influence was low and scattered before 2020, then triggered by Sailor’s career milestones.


Bottom chart — Other Genres Drawing from Oceanus Folk Over Time
Same structure but showing when OTHER genres started drawing from Oceanus Folk. The top chart shows when Oceanus Folk influenced others, the bottom shows when others started recognising and drawing back from Oceanus Folk. The start date difference is notable — the top chart starts from 2000 but the bottom starts from 2007, showing a 7-year lag before external genres began referencing Oceanus Folk. The 2028 peak on the bottom chart is even more pronounced than on the top — confirming Sailor’s breakthrough was the key trigger for external recognition.

title: Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread
text:
Top Influenced Artists



Top 15 artists most influenced by Oceanus Folk, ranked by number of influenced works and coloured by primary genre


A horizontal bar chart of the top 15 artists whose works were most influenced by Oceanus Folk, colored by their primary genre. Longer bar = more works influenced by Oceanus Folk. Each color represents the artist’s primary genre, showing which genre communities were most receptive to Oceanus Folk’s influence. The even bar lengths — most artists have 2–3 influenced works — confirm that Oceanus Folk’s impact was broadly distributed across many artists rather than concentrated on a few. Dream Pop and Desert Rock dominate the top 15, consistent with the genre-level findings in the Genre Influence Flow dashboard.

title: Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread
text:
Bidirectional Genre Flow (Sankey Diagram)



Oceanus Folk: Bidirectional Genre Influence Flow — Sankey diagram with outward (blue) and inward (purple) flows; orange nodes indicate bidirectional genres


A flow diagram showing the two-way musical influence between Oceanus Folk and other genres simultaneously. Left blue node (Oceanus Folk → Outward) represents Oceanus Folk sending influence to other genres. Right purple node (Inward → Oceanus Folk) represents other genres sending influence back to Oceanus Folk. Middle nodes are individual genres positioned between the two anchor nodes. Band thickness indicates strength — thicker bands = stronger influence relationship. Orange nodes indicate genres with two-way exchange (both receiving and giving influence). Light blue nodes are genres that only receive influence from Oceanus Folk. Red/pink nodes are genres that only give influence to Oceanus Folk.

title: Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread
text:
Key Analytical Findings







Question
Finding




Was influence gradual or intermittent?
Triggered — spiked dramatically in 2023 and 2028 aligned with Sailor’s career milestones


Which genres were most influenced?
Indie Folk (~100 connections), followed by Synthwave, Dream Pop and Doom Metal


Which artists were most influenced?
Influence broadly distributed across 483 artists — no single dominant recipient


What inspires Oceanus Folk?
Desert Rock, Dream Pop, Indie Folk and Space Rock are the strongest inbound influences


Is the exchange bidirectional?
Yes — Indie Folk, Desert Rock, Dream Pop and Space Rock all show two-way exchange


When did external recognition begin?
2007 — seven years after Oceanus Folk began referencing other genres in 2000

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text: Team: Radim Šoukal (01550535)  ·  Klara Kasparova (01550536)  ·  Charles-Antoine Tremblay (01550160)

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text:
Context
Oceanus, a small island nation once known primarily for its fishing communities, produced one of the most unlikely stories in modern music history. Sailor Shift — a vocalist who began her career in the niche Oceanus Folk genre — rose from regional obscurity to global recognition over the course of two decades. In 2023 she joined the Ivy Echoes, an all-female Oceanus Folk band, before her 2028 breakthrough single became the first Oceanus Folk song ever to top global charts. By 2040, journalist Silas Reed is writing a long-form article, “Oceanus Folk: Then and Now”, tracing this transformation and its lasting impact on the music world. To support his reporting, he has assembled a knowledge graph of artists, songs, albums, labels, and the influence relationships between them — and he needs visual analytics tools to make sense of it.
The MC1 knowledge graph contains 17,412 nodes across five types (Person, Song, Album, RecordLabel, MusicalGroup) and 37,857 directed edges across twelve relationship types, spanning nearly five decades from 1993 to 2040. This scale and relational complexity makes the dataset impossible to interpret through manual exploration or conventional tabular analysis. Purpose-built interactive visualisations are essential to surface the patterns of influence, collaboration, and genre evolution that Silas needs for his article.

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text:
Motivation & Objectives

Motivation
The fundamental challenge Silas Reed faces is one of scale and relational complexity. A knowledge graph with nearly 18,000 nodes and 38,000 edges cannot be meaningfully explored through tabular tools or manual browsing. At this size, patterns that span dozens or hundreds of relationships — such as how an artist’s stylistic choices propagated through a generation of subsequent musicians — are simply invisible without automated aggregation and visual encoding. The graph’s five node types and twelve edge types create a multi-relational structure where the same artist node may participate simultaneously in performer, composer, lyricist, producer, and influence relationships, each of which carries different analytical weight depending on the question being asked.
Beyond scale, the data has a temporal dimension that spans from 1993 to 2040 — nearly five decades — making longitudinal analysis essential. Musical influence is not static: artists who were obscure in one decade may become central nodes of influence in the next, and genres that once absorbed influence from Oceanus Folk may later feed inspiration back into it. Standard visualisation tools designed for snapshot data cannot capture this bidirectional, time-varying flow of influence. Finally, the multi-hop nature of influence propagation — where artist A shapes artist B, who in turn shapes artist C — requires graph traversal visualisation techniques that go beyond the capabilities of conventional business intelligence software. These three factors together make this project an ideal candidate for visual analytics.


Objectives

Task 1 — Sailor Shift Career Profile (Charles): Profile Sailor Shift’s career through interactive Tableau visualisations that map who influenced her and when, who she has collaborated with, and which artists she directly and indirectly shaped — tracing her impact on the broader Oceanus Folk community over time.
Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread (Klara): Trace the spread of Oceanus Folk as a genre by visualising how its influence expanded into adjacent genres such as Indie Folk, Dream Pop, Desert Rock, and Space Rock, whether that expansion was gradual or episodic, and how the genre itself has evolved by absorbing external influences since Sailor’s breakthrough.
Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction (Radim): Develop a data-driven profile of what characterises a breakout Oceanus Folk artist by examining the career trajectories of established rising stars, compare candidate artists against this benchmark, and name three evidence-based predictions for the next Oceanus Folk stars likely to emerge over the next five years.

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text:
Methodology
This project translates the MC1 knowledge graph into a suite of interactive Tableau dashboards, one per task, integrated into a single combined workbook. The raw graph data (MC1_graph.json) was first converted into flat CSV tables — separating node types and edge types — suitable for Tableau’s relational data model. Each sub-module then builds its visualisations on top of this prepared data.

Task 1 (Charles): Four interconnected dashboards — an influence timeline showing who shaped Sailor and when, a collaboration network graph centred on Sailor as the hub node, an indirect influence tree tracing second- and third-degree impacts, and a career tracker for her Ivy Echoes bandmates.
Task 2 (Klara): A Sankey-style genre flow diagram showing Oceanus Folk’s outward influence over time, a genre influence timeline chart from 1993 to 2040, a ranked bar chart of top influenced artists, and a genre inspiration heatmap showing which external genres have fed back into Oceanus Folk.
Task 3 (Radim): A radar chart defining the rising star profile across multiple metrics, a multi-line career arc comparison for three benchmark artists, a ranked candidate list with visual supporting evidence, and a prediction dashboard comparing top candidates against the rising star benchmark.

title: Task 1 — Sailor Shift Career Profile
section: Sub-question 1: Who has she been most influenced by over time?
text:
Sub-question 1: Who has she been most influenced by over time?
The bottom-left chart of the dashboard above maps Sailor Shift’s sources of inspiration across time. Each dot represents an influence relationship between one of Sailor’s works (X-axis = release year) and its source of inspiration (Y-axis = musical genre). The color indicates the type of influence.
Sailor Shift’s inspiration comes from a large sample of musical genres, 14 of them, ranging from Doom Metal to Synthwave and Indie Folk. From 2028, the year of her global breakthrough, influences diversified and intensified significantly. The dominant influence type is her getting inspired by the style of many genres, suggesting Sailor consistently reinterprets frameworks rather than copying existing works.
Undocumented inspirations are not reflected, which may underrepresent Sailor’s musical influences.
The dataset does not directly link her works to specific artists as sources of inspiration, only to songs, albums, and musical groups.
While it is possible to trace the performers and composers behind those works, this yields 87 largely anonymous individuals with no clear way to identify the most influential artists. As a result, this visualization answers ‘what genres’ influenced Sailor rather than ‘who specifically’ influenced her.

title: Task 1 — Sailor Shift Career Profile
section: Sub-question 2: Who has she collaborated with and directly or indirectly influenced?
text:
Sub-question 2: Who has she collaborated with and directly or indirectly influenced?

Collaborations
The top chart of the dashboard above shows all her collaborations with other artists across her career timeline and the type of collaboration. Each row represents a collaborator, each square the year of the collaboration, and the colors distinguish the different types. Multiple squares on the same line indicate a recurring collaboration.
From 2024 to 2026, her circle is tight and centered around Ivy Echoes members (Maya Jensen, Jade Thompson, Lila “Lilly” Hartman, and Sophie Ramirez), reflecting her early band period. The band then broke up, and she had a solo breakthrough year in 2028. From now on, an entirely new wave of collaborators emerges, growing and changing through the years. This suggests that Sailor’s rise attracted a more diverse network of collaborators, transitioning her from a band artist to a cooperative one.
The dataset only captures formal creative credits (composer, lyricist, performer), limiting the non-formal reality of collaborations across the music environment.


Influence
The bottom-right chart of the dashboard above identifies who Sailor has influenced. Each bar represents someone who referenced Sailor’s work, and the color distinguishes between direct influence (the entity referenced Sailor herself or her work) and indirect influence (the entity referenced Ivy Echoes or Sailor’s collaborators). The X-axis represents the number of times that each artist was linked to Sailor (documented).
Sailor Shift’s influence reaches 27 distinct entities from a wide range of genres including Oceanus Folk, Indie Rock, Jazz, and Surf Rock. The first four entities show both direct and indirect influences, suggesting that they are the most involved in Sailor’s artistic circle. The vast majority were influenced indirectly, highlighting that Sailor’s impact on the music world propagates mainly through her collaborative network rather than through direct artistic borrowing.
The scale of influence is limited to what is formally documented, many other artists around the world could have been influenced by her without expressing it directly.

section: Sub-question 3: How has she influenced collaborators of the broader Oceanus Folk community?
text:
Sub-question 3: How has she influenced collaborators of the broader Oceanus Folk community?



Oceanus Folk Production in Sailor Shift’s Circle Over Time (left) and Top Oceanus Folk Contributors in Sailor’s Circle After 2028 (right)



Left chart — Oceanus Folk Production Over Time
This chart tracks the volume of Oceanus Folk work produced annually within Sailor Shift’s circle. The vertical line marks 2028, the year of her breakthrough, and the colors distinguish three eras: before breakthrough, breakthrough year, and after breakthrough. Each bar represents the number of distinct Oceanus Folk works released each year.
The data tells a story of exponential growth. Before 2028, Oceanus Folk production was small and irregular, rarely exceeding 2 works per year. During the breakthrough year, production immediately jumps to 7 works, nearly doubling the previous year’s result. Post-2028, the number of works went up and peaked at 12 works in 2030, consistently remaining between 3 and 8 works per year. This pattern suggests that Sailor’s rise made her collaborators produce more Oceanus Folk music, creating a renaissance of the genre.
The broader Oceanus Folk community outside her immediate circle is not included, meaning that the full impact is likely to be greater than what is shown here.


Right chart — Top Contributors After 2028
This visualization complements the first one by ranking all Sailor’s related artists who produced 3 or more Oceanus Folk works after her breakthrough. Each bar represents an artist, and its length shows the total number of works they produced. The color distinguishes Ivy Echoes members from the other collaborators. Only the artists with at least 3 works are shown on the graph, unless they are Ivy members.
We can see that her former bandmates are among the least productive contributors, with only 2 works each. In contrast, new collaborators such as Beatrice Albright, Daniel O’Connell, and The Brine Choir lead the chart with 7 works each. This suggests that her influence on the Oceanus Folk community operated by attracting new artists rather than reactivating her original bandmates. The chart indicates that Sailor’s breakthrough created a wave of Oceanus Folk creativity beyond her founding circle.
The true community-wide impact is likely larger than what is shown since the visualization only includes artists directly connected to Sailor through some collaboration.

title: Write-up
section: Sub-question 1: Who has she been most influenced by over time?
text:
Sub-question 1: Who has she been most influenced by over time?
The bottom-left chart of the dashboard above maps Sailor Shift’s sources of inspiration across time. Each dot represents an influence relationship between one of Sailor’s works (X-axis = release year) and its source of inspiration (Y-axis = musical genre). The color indicates the type of influence.
Sailor Shift’s inspiration comes from a large sample of musical genres, 14 of them, ranging from Doom Metal to Synthwave and Indie Folk. From 2028, the year of her global breakthrough, influences diversified and intensified significantly. The dominant influence type is her getting inspired by the style of many genres, suggesting Sailor consistently reinterprets frameworks rather than copying existing works.
Undocumented inspirations are not reflected, which may underrepresent Sailor’s musical influences.
The dataset does not directly link her works to specific artists as sources of inspiration, only to songs, albums, and musical groups.
While it is possible to trace the performers and composers behind those works, this yields 87 largely anonymous individuals with no clear way to identify the most influential artists. As a result, this visualization answers ‘what genres’ influenced Sailor rather than ‘who specifically’ influenced her.

title: Write-up
section: Sub-question 2: Who has she collaborated with and directly or indirectly influenced?
text:
Sub-question 2: Who has she collaborated with and directly or indirectly influenced?

Collaborations
The top chart of the dashboard above shows all her collaborations with other artists across her career timeline and the type of collaboration. Each row represents a collaborator, each square the year of the collaboration, and the colors distinguish the different types. Multiple squares on the same line indicate a recurring collaboration.
From 2024 to 2026, her circle is tight and centered around Ivy Echoes members (Maya Jensen, Jade Thompson, Lila “Lilly” Hartman, and Sophie Ramirez), reflecting her early band period. The band then broke up, and she had a solo breakthrough year in 2028. From now on, an entirely new wave of collaborators emerges, growing and changing through the years. This suggests that Sailor’s rise attracted a more diverse network of collaborators, transitioning her from a band artist to a cooperative one.
The dataset only captures formal creative credits (composer, lyricist, performer), limiting the non-formal reality of collaborations across the music environment.


Influence
The bottom-right chart of the dashboard above identifies who Sailor has influenced. Each bar represents someone who referenced Sailor’s work, and the color distinguishes between direct influence (the entity referenced Sailor herself or her work) and indirect influence (the entity referenced Ivy Echoes or Sailor’s collaborators). The X-axis represents the number of times that each artist was linked to Sailor (documented).
Sailor Shift’s influence reaches 27 distinct entities from a wide range of genres including Oceanus Folk, Indie Rock, Jazz, and Surf Rock. The first four entities show both direct and indirect influences, suggesting that they are the most involved in Sailor’s artistic circle. The vast majority were influenced indirectly, highlighting that Sailor’s impact on the music world propagates mainly through her collaborative network rather than through direct artistic borrowing.
The scale of influence is limited to what is formally documented, many other artists around the world could have been influenced by her without expressing it directly.

text:
Genre Influence Flow



Genres Most Influenced by Oceanus Folk (left) and Genres That Inspire Oceanus Folk (right) — horizontal stacked bar charts coloured by edge type



Left chart — Genres Most Influenced by Oceanus Folk
A horizontal stacked bar chart showing target genres ranked by total connection count. Each color segment represents a different type of musical influence. Longer bar = stronger influence from Oceanus Folk on that genre. Each segment shows how the influence was expressed: CoverOf (Oceanus Folk songs were covered by this genre), DirectlySamples (Oceanus Folk audio was directly sampled), InStyleOf (works were created in the style of Oceanus Folk), InterpolatesFrom (melodies were borrowed from Oceanus Folk), LyricalReferenceTo (lyrics referenced Oceanus Folk works). The genres are sorted from most to least influenced — Indie Folk at the top is the most influenced genre.


Right chart — Genres That Inspire Oceanus Folk
Same chart structure but showing which genres feed INTO Oceanus Folk. If a genre appears prominently on both sides it means there is a two-way musical exchange with Oceanus Folk. Desert Rock, Dream Pop and Indie Folk appearing on both sides confirms they have a bidirectional relationship with Oceanus Folk.

title: Write-up
text:
Influence Through Time



Oceanus Folk Outward Influence Over Time (top) and Other Genres Drawing from Oceanus Folk Over Time (bottom) — both annotated with Sailor’s career milestones



Top chart — Oceanus Folk Outward Influence Over Time
A line chart where each point represents the number of influence connections made by Oceanus Folk works in that year. Higher points mean more Oceanus Folk works were influencing other genres that year. Two key events are marked directly on the chart: “Sailor joins Ivy Echoes” (2023) — marks the dramatic spike in outward influence; “Sailor’s global breakthrough” (2028) — marks the second wave of sustained influence. The overall pattern shows influence was low and scattered before 2020, then triggered by Sailor’s career milestones.


Bottom chart — Other Genres Drawing from Oceanus Folk Over Time
Same structure but showing when OTHER genres started drawing from Oceanus Folk. The top chart shows when Oceanus Folk influenced others, the bottom shows when others started recognising and drawing back from Oceanus Folk. The start date difference is notable — the top chart starts from 2000 but the bottom starts from 2007, showing a 7-year lag before external genres began referencing Oceanus Folk. The 2028 peak on the bottom chart is even more pronounced than on the top — confirming Sailor’s breakthrough was the key trigger for external recognition.

text:
Bidirectional Genre Flow (Sankey Diagram)



Oceanus Folk: Bidirectional Genre Influence Flow — Sankey diagram with outward (blue) and inward (purple) flows; orange nodes indicate bidirectional genres


A flow diagram showing the two-way musical influence between Oceanus Folk and other genres simultaneously. Left blue node (Oceanus Folk → Outward) represents Oceanus Folk sending influence to other genres. Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction

title: Write-up
text:
Visualisation — Tableau
Sheet 1 — OF Artist Ranking uses task3_artist_notoriety as its data source, with Performer Name on Rows, SUM(Total Notable Works) on Columns, and two filters applied: Is Oceanus Folk Artist = True and SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2. This produces a horizontal bar chart ranking all 14 Oceanus Folk artists with two or more charted works, sorted in descending order by total works. The chart establishes the candidate pool for rising star prediction: Orla Seabloom leads with 7 notable works, followed by Copper Canyon Ghosts (5), Daniel O’Connell (4), Beatrice Albright (4), and Ping Meng (4). The remaining nine artists each have exactly 2 notable works.
Sheet 2 — Genre Mix by Candidate uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Artist Name on Rows, CNT(Work Name) on Columns, and Genre on the Colour mark. A filter on CNT(Work Name) restricts the view to the top five candidates with the highest work counts. This stacked bar chart reveals the genre composition of each candidate’s notable output. All top candidates produce exclusively Oceanus Folk, with the exception of Ping Meng who has two Lo-Fi Electronica works and one Dream Pop work alongside a single Oceanus Folk work — identifying her as a genre-diverse outlier rather than a pure Oceanus Folk rising star.
Sheet 3 — Notoriety Timeline uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Notoriety Date on Columns (treated as a continuous dimension), Artist Name on Rows, Artist Name on the Colour mark, and Work Name on the Detail mark to plot one dot per notable work. A filter on SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2 retains the full 14-candidate pool. Rows are arranged so that artists with later career activity appear toward the top. This dot plot reveals the temporal distribution of each artist’s chart appearances — distinguishing between artists who achieved notoriety in a concentrated burst (Orla Seabloom with all 7 works charting in 2029, Beatrice Albright with 4 works in 2031) and those whose careers span many years (Ping Meng from 2011 to 2028, Tao Yin from 2013 to 2029).
Sheet 4 — Three Artist Comparison uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Notoriety Date on Columns (continuous), Artist Name and Work Name on Rows, and Artist Name on the Colour mark. An Artist Name filter selects only the three predicted rising stars: Copper Canyon Ghosts, Beatrice Albright, and Orla Seabloom. This sheet provides a detailed work-by-work comparison of each prediction’s career trajectory, showing the title and chart year of every notable work side by side.
Sheet 5 — Breakthrough Profile uses task3_artist_notoriety as its data source, with Years Active Span on Columns, SUM(Total Notable Works) on Rows, Performer Name on the Label mark, and a Circle mark type. Filters are Is Oceanus Folk Artist = True and SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2. The x-axis begins at 0. This scatter plot positions each of the 14 candidates by how long they were active before charting (x-axis) against how many works they have charted (y-axis). Artists in the top-left region — high output with a short active span — represent the ideal rising star profile. Orla Seabloom occupies this position most strongly (7 works, 0-year span), while Ping Meng sits as an extreme outlier in the top-right (4 works, 17-year span), indicating a long and slow career rather than a rapid breakout.

title: Write-up
text:
Visualisation — Python Radar Chart



Rising Star Profile — Oceanus Folk Candidates: radar chart comparing top 5 candidates across five normalised dimensions with companion data table


A radar chart was generated in Python using matplotlib (Data/task3_radar.py) to compare the top five Oceanus Folk candidates across five normalised dimensions simultaneously — a multi-axis comparison that cannot be achieved natively in Tableau.
The top five candidates by total_notable_works were selected from task3_artist_notoriety: Orla Seabloom (7), Copper Canyon Ghosts (5), Daniel O’Connell (4), Beatrice Albright (4), and Ping Meng (4).
Five metrics were defined and min–max normalised within this five-artist pool:








Metric
Field
Direction




Total Notable Works
total_notable_works
Higher = better


Quick Breakthrough
years_active_span (inverted)
Lower span = higher score


Early Breakthrough
first_notoriety_date (inverted)
Earlier year = higher score


Recent Activity
latest_notoriety_date
Later year = higher score


Genre Diversity
Count of unique genres in genres field
More genres = higher score



Normalisation was scoped to the five-candidate pool rather than the full dataset, so each radar axis represents relative standing among the top candidates. The chart was saved as a PNG at 150 dpi and embedded as a static image in the Tableau dashboard alongside a companion data table showing the raw values.

text:
Dashboards



Dashboard 1 — “Who are the Candidates?”: OF Artist Ranking (top-left), Genre Mix by Candidate (top-right), Breakthrough Profile scatter plot (bottom)


Dashboard 1 — “Who are the Candidates?” combines three sheets in a tiled layout: the OF Artist Ranking bar chart (top-left), the Genre Mix by Candidate stacked bar (top-right), and the Breakthrough Profile scatter plot (bottom, spanning full width). Together, these three views answer the question of which Oceanus Folk artists form the candidate pool and how they compare on output volume, genre focus, and breakthrough speed.



Dashboard 2 — “Who’s Next?”: Three Artist Comparison (top-left), Notoriety Timeline (bottom-left), radar chart and data table (right)


Dashboard 2 — “Who’s Next?” combines the Three Artist Comparison dot plot (top-left), the Notoriety Timeline (bottom-left), and the radar chart image (right). This dashboard presents the final predictions. The Three Artist Comparison provides a detailed work-by-work timeline for the three predicted stars, the Notoriety Timeline provides broader context by showing all 14 candidates, and the radar chart synthesises the five key metrics into a single comparative profile for the top five.

title: Write-up
text:
Results and Discussion

What does it mean to be a rising star?
Based on the visualisations above, a rising star in the Oceanus Folk music scene is characterised by five attributes:

High notable output — the artist has multiple works that achieved chart notoriety, not just a single hit. Among the 56 Oceanus Folk artists in the dataset, only 14 produced two or more notable works, and the strongest candidates produced four or more.
Rapid breakthrough — the time between the artist’s first release and their first chart appearance is short. Orla Seabloom and Beatrice Albright both have a years-active span of zero, meaning their works charted in the same year they were released. Copper Canyon Ghosts achieved a span of just one year. By contrast, Ping Meng’s 17-year span and Tao Yin’s 16-year span suggest established careers rather than breakout trajectories.
Concentrated momentum — rising stars tend to chart multiple works within a narrow time window rather than sporadically over many years. Orla Seabloom charted all 7 works in 2029; Beatrice Albright charted all 4 in 2031. This burst pattern signals a career inflection point — the hallmark of a rising star.
Genre commitment — the strongest Oceanus Folk candidates produce almost exclusively within the genre. Ping Meng, despite having 4 notable works, splits across Lo-Fi Electronica, Dream Pop, and Oceanus Folk — making her more of a genre-crossing veteran than a rising Oceanus Folk star.
Recency — artists whose chart activity is more recent are more likely to represent the next wave. Copper Canyon Ghosts’ notoriety dates (2034–2035) are the most recent in the candidate pool, placing them at the frontier of the genre’s evolution.

The Breakthrough Profile scatter plot captures attributes 1 and 2 directly: ideal candidates cluster in the top-left (high works, low span). The Notoriety Timeline captures attribute 3 visually through dot clustering. The Genre Mix chart captures attribute 4 through colour segmentation. The radar chart synthesises all five attributes into a single comparative shape — a larger, more balanced polygon indicates a stronger overall rising star profile.


Three Artist Comparison
The three artists selected for detailed comparison are Orla Seabloom, Beatrice Albright, and Copper Canyon Ghosts. Each represents a distinct breakout pattern:
Orla Seabloom had the most explosive debut in the candidate pool — all 7 of her notable works (Beneath the Whispering Waves, Ebbing Heart, Riding the Moon’s Reflection, Seasheel Lullaby, The Shifting Sands, The Song of Forgotten Shores, and Tidal Journey) charted in a single year (2029). Her radar profile is the largest overall shape, dominating the Total Notable Works axis and scoring maximally on Quick Breakthrough. However, because all works charted in the same year, her Recent Activity score is moderate — her last chart appearance was 2029, earlier than the other two predictions.
Beatrice Albright followed a similar concentrated pattern, charting 4 works (Moonlit Kelp Forest Dance, Seven Fathoms Deep, The Siren’s Sigh, Whispers on the Jetty Wind) in 2031, all in Oceanus Folk. Her years-active span is also zero, giving her a strong Quick Breakthrough score. Her profile closely mirrors Orla Seabloom’s shape on the radar chart, albeit smaller due to fewer total works.
Copper Canyon Ghosts charted 5 works across 2034–2035 (Canyon Echoes, Ocean Dreams; Crimson Cliffs Awakening; Salt-Stained Letters; Shadows in Smuggler’s Cove; The Cartographer’s Curse), all in Oceanus Folk. With only a 1-year span and the most recent notoriety dates in the entire candidate pool, Copper Canyon Ghosts score highest on Recent Activity. Their radar profile is the most balanced of the three, with strong scores across all axes except Early Breakthrough (since their career began later).


Predictions — Next Three Oceanus Folk Stars
Based on the rising star profile defined above, the three artists predicted to be the next Oceanus Folk breakout stars over the next five years are:

Copper Canyon Ghosts — Their 5 notable works between 2034 and 2035, combined with a 1-year active span and pure Oceanus Folk output, make them the most recent breakout act in the candidate pool. Recency is a decisive factor for a forward-looking prediction: their chart activity is the newest of any candidate, giving them the strongest momentum heading into the next five years.
Orla Seabloom — With 7 notable works and an instant breakthrough in 2029, she has the highest volume and fastest rise of any Oceanus Folk candidate. Her concentrated output suggests an artist at the peak of a career inflection, and her exclusive focus on Oceanus Folk makes her the genre’s most prolific emerging voice. She ranks second because her chart activity, while impressive, is five years older than Copper Canyon Ghosts’.
Beatrice Albright — With 4 works charting simultaneously in 2031 and zero years-active span, she demonstrates the same explosive breakout pattern as Orla Seabloom. Her exclusively Oceanus Folk catalogue and rapid rise make her a strong candidate to sustain chart presence in the coming years.

These three were chosen over Daniel O’Connell (who matches Beatrice Albright’s numbers but shares the same 2031 burst, making them interchangeable on the available metrics) and Ping Meng (whose 17-year span and multi-genre output disqualify her from the rising star profile despite having 4 notable works).

The top chart shows when Oceanus Folk influenced others, the bottom shows when others started recognising and drawing back from Oceanus Folk. The start date difference is notable — the top chart starts from 2000 but the bottom starts from 2007, showing a 7-year lag before external genres began referencing Oceanus Folk. The 2028 peak on the bottom chart is even more pronounced than on the top — confirming Sailor’s breakthrough was the key trigger for external recognition.

Top Influenced Artists



Top 15 artists most influenced by Oceanus Folk, ranked by number of influenced works and coloured by primary genre


A horizontal bar chart of the top 15 artists whose works were most influenced by Oceanus Folk, colored by their primary genre. Longer bar = more works influenced by Oceanus Folk. Each color represents the artist’s primary genre, showing which genre communities were most receptive to Oceanus Folk’s influence. title: Write-up
text:
Bidirectional Genre Flow (Sankey Diagram)



Oceanus Folk: Bidirectional Genre Influence Flow — Sankey diagram with outward (blue) and inward (purple) flows; orange nodes indicate bidirectional genres


A flow diagram showing the two-way musical influence between Oceanus Folk and other genres simultaneously. Left blue node (Oceanus Folk → Outward) represents Oceanus Folk sending influence to other genres. Right purple node (Inward → Oceanus Folk) represents other genres sending influence back to Oceanus Folk. Middle nodes are individual genres positioned between the two anchor nodes. Band thickness indicates strength — thicker bands = stronger influence relationship. Orange nodes indicate genres with two-way exchange (both receiving and giving influence). Light blue nodes are genres that only receive influence from Oceanus Folk. Red/pink nodes are genres that only give influence to Oceanus Folk.

Key Analytical Findings







Question
Finding




Was influence gradual or intermittent?
Triggered — spiked dramatically in 2023 and 2028 aligned with Sailor’s career milestones


Which genres were most influenced?
Indie Folk (~100 connections), followed by Synthwave, Dream Pop and Doom Metal


Which artists were most influenced?
Influence broadly distributed across 483 artists — no single dominant recipient


What inspires Oceanus Folk?
Desert Rock, Dream Pop, Indie Folk and Space Rock are the strongest inbound influences


Is the exchange bidirectional?
Yes — Indie Folk, Desert Rock, Dream Pop and Space Rock all show two-way exchange


When did external recognition begin?
2007 — seven years after Oceanus Folk began referencing other genres in 2000

title: Write-up
text:
Visualisation — Tableau
Sheet 1 — OF Artist Ranking uses task3_artist_notoriety as its data source, with Performer Name on Rows, SUM(Total Notable Works) on Columns, and two filters applied: Is Oceanus Folk Artist = True and SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2. This produces a horizontal bar chart ranking all 14 Oceanus Folk artists with two or more charted works, sorted in descending order by total works. The chart establishes the candidate pool for rising star prediction: Orla Seabloom leads with 7 notable works, followed by Copper Canyon Ghosts (5), Daniel O’Connell (4), Beatrice Albright (4), and Ping Meng (4). The remaining nine artists each have exactly 2 notable works.
Sheet 2 — Genre Mix by Candidate uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Artist Name on Rows, CNT(Work Name) on Columns, and Genre on the Colour mark. A filter on CNT(Work Name) restricts the view to the top five candidates with the highest work counts. This stacked bar chart reveals the genre composition of each candidate’s notable output. All top candidates produce exclusively Oceanus Folk, with the exception of Ping Meng who has two Lo-Fi Electronica works and one Dream Pop work alongside a single Oceanus Folk work — identifying her as a genre-diverse outlier rather than a pure Oceanus Folk rising star.
Sheet 3 — Notoriety Timeline uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Notoriety Date on Columns (treated as a continuous dimension), Artist Name on Rows, Artist Name on the Colour mark, and Work Name on the Detail mark to plot one dot per notable work. A filter on SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2 retains the full 14-candidate pool. Rows are arranged so that artists with later career activity appear toward the top. This dot plot reveals the temporal distribution of each artist’s chart appearances — distinguishing between artists who achieved notoriety in a concentrated burst (Orla Seabloom with all 7 works charting in 2029, Beatrice Albright with 4 works in 2031) and those whose careers span many years (Ping Meng from 2011 to 2028, Tao Yin from 2013 to 2029).
Sheet 4 — Three Artist Comparison uses task3_of_candidates as its data source, with Notoriety Date on Columns (continuous), Artist Name and Work Name on Rows, and Artist Name on the Colour mark. An Artist Name filter selects only the three predicted rising stars: Copper Canyon Ghosts, Beatrice Albright, and Orla Seabloom. This sheet provides a detailed work-by-work comparison of each prediction’s career trajectory, showing the title and chart year of every notable work side by side.
Sheet 5 — Breakthrough Profile uses task3_artist_notoriety as its data source, with Years Active Span on Columns, SUM(Total Notable Works) on Rows, Performer Name on the Label mark, and a Circle mark type. Filters are Is Oceanus Folk Artist = True and SUM(Total Notable Works) >= 2. The x-axis begins at 0. This scatter plot positions each of the 14 candidates by how long they were active before charting (x-axis) against how many works they have charted (y-axis). Artists in the top-left region — high output with a short active span — represent the ideal rising star profile. Orla Seabloom occupies this position most strongly (7 works, 0-year span), while Ping Meng sits as an extreme outlier in the top-right (4 works, 17-year span), indicating a long and slow career rather than a rapid breakout.

title: Write-up
text:
Results and Discussion

What does it mean to be a rising star?
Based on the visualisations above, a rising star in the Oceanus Folk music scene is characterised by five attributes:

High notable output — the artist has multiple works that achieved chart notoriety, not just a single hit. Among the 56 Oceanus Folk artists in the dataset, only 14 produced two or more notable works, and the strongest candidates produced four or more.
Rapid breakthrough — the time between the artist’s first release and their first chart appearance is short. Orla Seabloom and Beatrice Albright both have a years-active span of zero, meaning their works charted in the same year they were released. Copper Canyon Ghosts achieved a span of just one year. By contrast, Ping Meng’s 17-year span and Tao Yin’s 16-year span suggest established careers rather than breakout trajectories.
Concentrated momentum — rising stars tend to chart multiple works within a narrow time window rather than sporadically over many years. Orla Seabloom charted all 7 works in 2029; Beatrice Albright charted all 4 in 2031. This burst pattern signals a career inflection point — the hallmark of a rising star.
Genre commitment — the strongest Oceanus Folk candidates produce almost exclusively within the genre. Ping Meng, despite having 4 notable works, splits across Lo-Fi Electronica, Dream Pop, and Oceanus Folk — making her more of a genre-crossing veteran than a rising Oceanus Folk star.
Recency — artists whose chart activity is more recent are more likely to represent the next wave. Copper Canyon Ghosts’ notoriety dates (2034–2035) are the most recent in the candidate pool, placing them at the frontier of the genre’s evolution.

The Breakthrough Profile scatter plot captures attributes 1 and 2 directly: ideal candidates cluster in the top-left (high works, low span). The Notoriety Timeline captures attribute 3 visually through dot clustering. The Genre Mix chart captures attribute 4 through colour segmentation. The radar chart synthesises all five attributes into a single comparative shape — a larger, more balanced polygon indicates a stronger overall rising star profile.


Three Artist Comparison
The three artists selected for detailed comparison are Orla Seabloom, Beatrice Albright, and Copper Canyon Ghosts. Each represents a distinct breakout pattern:
Orla Seabloom had the most explosive debut in the candidate pool — all 7 of her notable works (Beneath the Whispering Waves, Ebbing Heart, Riding the Moon’s Reflection, Seasheel Lullaby, The Shifting Sands, The Song of Forgotten Shores, and Tidal Journey) charted in a single year (2029). Her radar profile is the largest overall shape, dominating the Total Notable Works axis and scoring maximally on Quick Breakthrough. However, because all works charted in the same year, her Recent Activity score is moderate — her last chart appearance was 2029, earlier than the other two predictions.
Beatrice Albright followed a similar concentrated pattern, charting 4 works (Moonlit Kelp Forest Dance, Seven Fathoms Deep, The Siren’s Sigh, Whispers on the Jetty Wind) in 2031, all in Oceanus Folk. Her years-active span is also zero, giving her a strong Quick Breakthrough score. Her profile closely mirrors Orla Seabloom’s shape on the radar chart, albeit smaller due to fewer total works.
Copper Canyon Ghosts charted 5 works across 2034–2035 (Canyon Echoes, Ocean Dreams; Crimson Cliffs Awakening; Salt-Stained Letters; Shadows in Smuggler’s Cove; The Cartographer’s Curse), all in Oceanus Folk. With only a 1-year span and the most recent notoriety dates in the entire candidate pool, Copper Canyon Ghosts score highest on Recent Activity. Their radar profile is the most balanced of the three, with strong scores across all axes except Early Breakthrough (since their career began later).


Predictions — Next Three Oceanus Folk Stars
Based on the rising star profile defined above, the three artists predicted to be the next Oceanus Folk breakout stars over the next five years are:

Copper Canyon Ghosts — Their 5 notable works between 2034 and 2035, combined with a 1-year active span and pure Oceanus Folk output, make them the most recent breakout act in the candidate pool. Recency is a decisive factor for a forward-looking prediction: their chart activity is the newest of any candidate, giving them the strongest momentum heading into the next five years.
Orla Seabloom — With 7 notable works and an instant breakthrough in 2029, she has the highest volume and fastest rise of any Oceanus Folk candidate. Her concentrated output suggests an artist at the peak of a career inflection, and her exclusive focus on Oceanus Folk makes her the genre’s most prolific emerging voice. She ranks second because her chart activity, while impressive, is five years older than Copper Canyon Ghosts’.
Beatrice Albright — With 4 works charting simultaneously in 2031 and zero years-active span, she demonstrates the same explosive breakout pattern as Orla Seabloom. Her exclusively Oceanus Folk catalogue and rapid rise make her a strong candidate to sustain chart presence in the coming years.

These three were chosen over Daniel O’Connell (who matches Beatrice Albright’s numbers but shares the same 2031 burst, making them interchangeable on the available metrics) and Ping Meng (whose 17-year span and multi-genre output disqualify her from the rising star profile despite having 4 notable works).

title: User Guide
text: This guide explains how to interact with each Tableau dashboard and Python-generated visualisation on this website. Each section covers what the chart shows, how to read it, and what interactions are available.

title: User Guide
text:
Task 1 — Sailor Shift Career Profile

Dashboard 1 — Sailor’s Influence & Collaborations
This dashboard combines three charts: Collaborations Over Time (top), Genre Influences (bottom-left), and Direct & Indirect Influence (bottom-right).
Top chart — Sailor Shift’s Collaborations Over Time. Each row is a collaborator, each square is the year of a collaboration, and the colour indicates the collaboration type (Composer, Lyricist, Performer, Producer). Multiple squares on the same row mean the collaborator worked with Sailor more than once. Hover over any square to see the collaborator name, work title, and release year. Use the Ivy Echoes Members filter on the right to isolate only Ivy Echoes bandmates and compare their activity against the broader collaborator pool. The Ivy filter toggles between 0 (non-members) and 1 (members).
Bottom-left chart — What Genres Has Influenced Sailor Shift The Most? Each dot represents one influence relationship between a Sailor work (X-axis = release year) and its source genre (Y-axis). Colour indicates the influence type: Cover, Direct Sample, Inspired by Style, Lyrical Reference, or Melody Interpolation. Hover over any dot for the exact work name and genre. The filter on Sailor Work Release Year lets you narrow the time range.
Bottom-right chart — Direct & Indirect Influence. Each bar represents a person or group that referenced Sailor’s work or her collaborators’ work. Bar length shows the number of documented influence connections. Hover over any bar segment to see the entity name and influence type breakdown.


Dashboard 2 — Sailor’s Impact on Oceanus Folk
This dashboard combines two charts: Oceanus Folk Production Over Time (left) and Top Contributors After 2028 (right).
Left chart — Oceanus Folk Production in Sailor Shift’s Circle Over Time. Each bar represents the number of distinct Oceanus Folk works released that year within Sailor’s collaborative network. The vertical dashed line marks 2028 (her breakthrough). Colours distinguish three eras: Before Breakthrough (blue), Breakthrough Year (orange), and After Breakthrough (dark grey). Hover over any bar for the exact count and year.
Right chart — Top Oceanus Folk Contributors in Sailor’s Circle After 2028. A horizontal bar chart ranking artists by the number of OF works they produced after 2028. Colour distinguishes Ivy Echoes members (purple) from other collaborators (grey). The Era filter is pre-set to “After Breakthrough” — this filter controls both charts, so changing it will update the left chart as well. The Artist Name filter restricts which artists appear.


Interactive Network Graphs (Python)
Two interactive HTML network graphs are embedded below the Tableau dashboards on the Task 1 analysis page.
Collaboration Network. An undirected graph showing Sailor Shift (gold node) at the centre, connected to her direct collaborators (blue nodes) and Ivy Echoes members (green nodes). Each edge represents a shared work — hover over an edge to see the collaboration types. Click any node to highlight only its immediate connections. Drag nodes to rearrange the layout. The graph uses a force-directed physics simulation that stabilises automatically.
Influence Network. A directed graph showing the full influence chain: Sailor (gold star), her works (orange dots), collaborators (blue), Ivy Echoes members (green), and artists inspired by Sailor or her circle (purple). Arrows indicate the direction of influence. Click any node to highlight its neighbourhood. Hover for details including genre and release year.

title: User Guide
text:
Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread

Dashboard 1 — Genre Influence Flow
This dashboard shows which genres Oceanus Folk most influenced (left chart) and which genres most inspired Oceanus Folk (right chart).
Left chart — Genres Most Influenced by Oceanus Folk. A horizontal stacked bar chart showing target genres ranked by total connection count. Each colour segment represents a different type of musical influence: CoverOf, DirectlySamples, InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, and LyricalReferenceTo. Longer bar = stronger influence from Oceanus Folk on that genre. Hover over any bar segment to see the exact connection count for that edge type and genre. The genres are sorted from most to least influenced.

Dashboard 2 — Influence Through Time
This dashboard shows how Oceanus Folk’s influence evolved over time — both outward and inward.
Top chart — By how many genres was Oceanus Folk influenced over time. A line chart where each point represents the number of influence connections made by Oceanus Folk works in that year. Higher points mean more Oceanus Folk works were influencing other genres that year. Two key events are annotated on the chart: “Sailor joins Ivy Echoes” (2023) and “Sailor’s global breakthrough” (2028). Hover over any data point to see the exact year and connection count.
Bottom chart — How many genres were influenced by Oceanus Folk over time. Same structure but showing when other genres started drawing from Oceanus Folk. Compare with the top chart to see the temporal lag between Oceanus Folk producing influence and other genres responding.


Dashboard 3 — Top Influenced Artists
Chart — Top Artists Influenced by Oceanus Folk. A horizontal bar chart of the top 15 artists whose works were most influenced by Oceanus Folk, coloured by their primary genre. Longer bar = more works influenced by Oceanus Folk. Hover over any bar to see the artist name, exact influenced works count, and primary genre. The Artist Name filter at the top restricts which artists appear.


Sankey Diagram (Python)
An interactive HTML version of the Sankey diagram is available at images/task2/task2_sankey_genre_flow_python.html — open it in any web browser. A static PNG is embedded on the analysis page.
How to read it. The left blue node (“Oceanus Folk → Outward”) represents Oceanus Folk sending influence to other genres. The right purple node (“Inward → Oceanus Folk”) represents other genres sending influence back. Middle nodes are individual genres. Band thickness indicates the strength of the relationship. Orange nodes appear on both sides (bidirectional influence). Light blue nodes only receive influence from Oceanus Folk. Red/pink nodes only give influence to Oceanus Folk.
How to interact. Hover over any band to see the exact genre name and connection count. Hover over any node to see the total flow volume. Click and drag any node to reposition it on the canvas for easier reading of overlapping bands.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Sailor Shift is an Oceanus Folk artist who co-founded the Ivy Echoes in 2023 before achieving a viral breakthrough in 2028 — the first Oceanus Folk artist to top global charts. Since then, she has released acclaimed albums annually, collaborated widely across genres, and actively mentored emerging artists, while her former bandmates have independently contributed to the spread of Oceanus Folk across the music world.
Understanding this network of influence is the core challenge. Journalist Silas Reed has compiled a knowledge graph of 17,412 nodes and 37,857 edges spanning music history from 1993 to 2040. While this dataset contains all the answers needed for his article “Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now”, raw graph data is unreadable without purpose-built visualisation. Tracing Sailor’s influences, measuring how Oceanus Folk spread into other genres, or identifying the next rising star cannot be done by browsing a JSON file.
By transforming this knowledge graph into interactive Tableau dashboards, we turn a complex relational dataset into clear, navigable insights about how musical influence works and how stars are made.




Task 1 — Career Profile: Design Tableau visualisations to map Sailor Shift’s influences, collaborations, and the direct and indirect impact she has had on other artists and on the Oceanus Folk community. This includes tracing who shaped her sound and when, identifying her key collaborators, and visualising the downstream influence chain.
Task 2 — Genre Spread: Develop visualisations showing how Oceanus Folk’s influence has spread across the music world since Sailor’s breakthrough, which genres it most affected (Indie Folk, Dream Pop, Desert Rock, Space Rock, Synthwave, etc.), and how the genre itself has evolved through external influences, making visible the flow between Oceanus Folk and the broader musical ecosystem.
Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction: Build a data-driven profile of what constitutes a breakout Oceanus Folk artist, compare the career trajectories of three benchmark artists, and provide three predictions for the next stars likely to emerge over the next five years, drawing on the notable works and notoriety date data as the primary signal of an artist’s rising trajectory.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Data
The datasets for this project are mc1_nodes.csv and mc1_edges.csv, derived from MC1_graph.json provided by VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1. They represent the music industry in and around Oceanus from 1993 to 2040, containing 17,412 nodes and 37,857 edges across five node types and twelve relationship types. Edge types capture creative relationships including performance, composition, production, and stylistic influence — with the influence category (InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, CoverOf, DirectlySamples) forming the primary analytical layer for tracing how musical ideas spread between artists and genres.
Two fields are critical for Task 3. The notable boolean records whether a work charted, and notoriety_date records when — together allowing reconstruction of each artist’s breakthrough trajectory across 649 charted works.

title: Methodology
text: This project combines two deliverables — a Tableau application and this Quarto website — to address the three tasks of VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1. The raw source data (MC1_graph.json) was transformed into task-specific CSV files using a Python preprocessing pipeline (Data/preprocess_mc1.py), which were then loaded into Tableau for interactive dashboard construction; three supplementary chart types not natively supported by Tableau (network graphs, Sankey diagram, radar chart) were produced in Python and embedded in the website.

Two fields are critical for the analysis. The boolean notable records whether a song or album appeared on a top chart. The string field notoriety_date records the year of that first chart appearance. Together these allow reconstruction of each artist’s breakthrough trajectory across 649 charted works.


Data Preparation
A single Python script (Data/preprocess_mc1.py) transforms the raw node and edge CSVs into 14 task-specific files stored in Data/mc1_csv/processed/. The script begins by loading mc1_nodes.csv (17,412 rows) and mc1_edges.csv (37,857 rows) and applying a uniform cleaning pass: notable and single fields have NaN values filled as False and are cast to boolean; release_date, notoriety_date, and written_date are converted from float to integer-year strings with missing values stored as empty strings; genre is filled as "Unknown" where missing; stage_name is filled as empty string; name is whitespace-stripped; and all ID columns (id, source, target) are cast to integer for reliable merge keys. Twelve edge types are partitioned into two semantic groups used throughout the pipeline: five influence types (InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, CoverOf, DirectlySamples, LyricalReferenceTo) and four collaboration types (PerformerOf, ComposerOf, LyricistOf, ProducerOf). A helper function computes each artist’s main genre as the most frequent genre among all works they are connected to via collaboration edges; this derived attribute is reused across multiple output files.
For Task 1, the script identifies Sailor Shift by node ID 17255 and her Ivy Echoes bandmates by IDs 17256–17259 (Maya Jensen, Lila Hartman, Jade Thompson, Sophie Ramirez), with the Ivy Echoes group entity at ID 17260. task1_a_who_inspired_sailor.csv (30 rows) captures all influence edges originating from Sailor’s 38 works and from Sailor as a person, with targets representing the songs, albums, or groups that inspired her; edge type codes are mapped to human-readable names (e.g. InStyleOf → Inspired by Style) and a Source Is Person flag distinguishes whether the influence link originated from a specific work or from Sailor directly. task1_b_sailor_collaborations.csv (126 rows) collects every collaboration edge on Sailor’s 38 works — not just Sailor’s own edges but all persons and groups who performed, composed, wrote lyrics for, or produced those works — enriched with each person’s computed main genre and binary flags for Ivy Echoes membership and Sailor identity. task1_c_oceanus_impact_timeline.csv (215 rows) focuses on Sailor’s local Oceanus Folk network: it takes a curated set of 81 OF works connected to Sailor’s circle, identifies all artists on those works whose main genre falls within the OF-related family (Oceanus Folk, Celtic Folk, Sea Shanties, Acoustic Folk), and labels each collaboration with an Era column based on whether the work was released before, during, or after Sailor’s breakthrough year of 2028. All three Task 1 files are saved as UTF-16 tab-separated CSVs to match the format expected by the existing Tableau workbook.
For Task 2, the script collects all Oceanus Folk song and album node IDs, then extracts two directional influence sets. task2_of_influence_out.csv (371 rows) captures all influence edges where the source is an OF work, joined with source and target node metadata (name, genre, release date, notable status, notoriety date). task2_of_influence_in.csv (377 rows) captures the reverse — influence edges where the target is an OF work. task2_genre_summary.csv (117 rows) aggregates both sets by (source_genre, target_genre, edge_type) with a direction column (outbound or inbound), excluding internal OF-to-OF flows. task2_top_artists_influenced.csv (30 rows) traces PerformerOf edges from the non-OF works that appear as targets in the outbound influence set back to their performing artists, aggregates per artist the count of influenced works and notable works among them, assigns a primary genre based on the most frequent genre among the artist’s influenced works, and exports the top 30 artists ranked by influenced work count.
For Task 3, task3_notable_works.csv (1,973 rows) filters all nodes where notable = True and notoriety_date is not empty, then joins PerformerOf edges to associate each charted work with its performing artist. task3_artist_notoriety.csv (1,664 rows) aggregates to one row per performer with four summary metrics — total_notable_works, first_notoriety_date, latest_notoriety_date, and years_active_span (difference between earliest and latest release dates) — plus a comma-separated genres field and a boolean is_oceanus_folk_artist flag set to True if any of the artist’s notable works are Oceanus Folk. task3_of_candidates.csv (84 rows) filters to OF artists only and joins the four summary metrics from the notoriety table, enabling both work-level and artist-level analysis within a single Tableau data source.
Finally, two shared lookup files are produced: lookup_nodes_clean.csv (17,412 rows) exports the full cleaned node table, and lookup_edges_clean.csv (37,857 rows) exports the full edge table enriched with source and target node names and types. Two additional files — nodes.csv (193 rows) and edges.csv (340 rows) — provide a curated subgraph of Sailor Shift’s local network used as input by the Python network visualisation scripts; nodes are classified into roles (Sailor Shift, Ivy Echoes Member, Direct Collaborator, Sailor Work, Inspired Sailor, Inspired By Sailor Circle) and edges carry an edge_category label and involvement flags.

title: Methodology
text:
Tools
Tableau handles all standard chart types: horizontal and stacked bar charts, scatter plots, dot plots, line charts, and highlight tables. Every interactive dashboard was built in Tableau and exported as a combined .twbx workbook. All CSV files were loaded as separate data connections within a single workbook — no cross-source joins or unions were performed to avoid row multiplication and direction mixing. Each sheet uses only one data source at a time.
Python was used for three chart types that Tableau does not natively support:

Network graphs (Task 1): Built using pyvis (a Python wrapper around vis.js). Two interactive HTML network visualisations — a collaboration network and an influence network — were generated and embedded in the Quarto website via <iframe> elements. Pyvis was selected because it produces self-contained HTML files with built-in pan, zoom, and hover interactions, which can be embedded directly in a static website without a server backend.
Sankey diagram (Task 2): Built using plotly to show bidirectional genre influence flows. Plotly’s Sankey trace type was selected because it handles variable-width flow bands with hover tooltips and node repositioning out of the box. The output was saved as both interactive HTML and a static PNG for embedding.
Radar chart (Task 3): Built using matplotlib to compare five normalised metrics across the top five Oceanus Folk candidates simultaneously. Matplotlib’s polar projection was used because it provides precise control over grid lines, label positioning, and overlapping polygon fills — features that Plotly’s Scatterpolar lacks at equivalent visual polish. The output was saved as a static PNG at 150 dpi and embedded as an image in the Tableau dashboard.

Quarto + Vercel powers this website. Source files are .qmd documents rendered to HTML by Quarto and auto-deployed to production on every push to the main branch via Vercel.

title: Methodology
text:
Python Visualisation — Task 1 Network Graphs
Two interactive network graphs were generated using pyvis (Python) and embedded in the Quarto website as HTML <iframe> elements.
Collaboration Network (Data/task1_network_collaboration.py): The script loads the processed node and edge CSVs, identifies all Person nodes with roles “Sailor Shift”, “Direct Collaborator”, or “Ivy Echoes Member”, then constructs derived edges by finding collaborators who share work nodes with Sailor. For each collaborator, the edge types (e.g. PerformerOf, ComposerOf) are aggregated into a tooltip. The network uses a ForceAtlas2 physics layout with gravitational constant −80, spring length 120, and 250 stabilisation iterations. Node colours distinguish Sailor (gold, size 40), Ivy Echoes members (emerald green, size 25), and other collaborators (blue, size 20). A fixed title bar and colour legend were injected into the output HTML.
Influence Network (Data/task1_network_influence.py): The script includes six node roles: Sailor Shift, Sailor’s works, direct collaborators, Ivy Echoes members, artists inspired by Sailor, and artists inspired by Sailor’s circle. Directed edges preserve the original edge types. The layout uses ForceAtlas2 with gravitational constant −60, spring length 150, and 300 stabilisation iterations. Sailor is rendered as a gold star (size 50), her works as orange dots, collaborators as blue dots, Ivy Echoes members as green dots, and inspired artists as purple dots.

title: Methodology
text:
Python Visualisation — Task 2 Sankey Diagram
A bidirectional Sankey diagram was generated using plotly to show the flow of influence between Oceanus Folk and other genres simultaneously. The script loads task2_genre_summary.csv, removes internal Oceanus Folk flows and connections with fewer than 3 occurrences, then creates two virtual anchor nodes: “Oceanus Folk → Outward” (left, blue) and “Inward → Oceanus Folk” (right, purple). Genre nodes are positioned between the anchors and colour-coded: orange for bidirectional genres (those appearing on both outbound and inbound sides), light blue for outbound-only, and red/pink for inbound-only. The output was saved as both an interactive HTML file and a static PNG.

title: Methodology
text:
Python Visualisation — Task 3 Radar Chart
A radar chart (Data/task3_radar.py) compares the top five Oceanus Folk candidates across five normalised dimensions. The script loads task3_artist_notoriety.csv, filters to Oceanus Folk artists, selects the top five by total_notable_works, and computes a genre diversity count from the comma-separated genres field.
Five metrics are defined and min–max normalised within the five-candidate pool:








Metric
Source Field
Direction




Total Notable Works
total_notable_works
Higher = better


Quick Breakthrough
years_active_span (inverted)
Lower span = higher score


Early Breakthrough
first_notoriety_date (inverted)
Earlier year = higher score


Recent Activity
latest_notoriety_date
Later year = higher score


Genre Diversity
Count of unique genres in genres
More genres = higher score



Normalisation is scoped to the five-candidate pool rather than the full dataset, so each radar axis represents relative standing among the top candidates only. The chart uses matplotlib’s polar projection with a white background, five concentric grid rings (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00), spoke lines, and semi-transparent filled polygons for each artist. A companion data table showing raw values is rendered alongside the radar using matplotlib’s table function. Specific colours were assigned to each artist for consistency with the Tableau dashboards. The figure was saved at 150 dpi as a PNG and embedded in the Tableau workbook as a static image.
